title: Revisiting the luminescence properties of Pr3+: YAG within the framework of an extended approach of Judd-Ofelt theory
authors: Maxence Lepers, G. Hovhannesyan, Y. Guyot, R. Moncorgé, M. Velazquez
abstract: We show in this article the improvements which can be obtained in the description of the luminescence properties of Pr3+ doped materials by using an extension of the Judd-Ofelt theory in order to relax some strong selection rules and approximations of the standard formalism and to better account for the influence of the 4f5d excited electronic configuration. The demonstration is made by re-examining the case of Pr3+:YAG, a well known luminescent and laser crystal with a very low energy 4f5d absorption band. Our extension thus provides a better agreement between calculated and measured absorption intensities, especially for the hypersensitive 3 H4 $\rightarrow$ 3 P2 transition. A comparison is made with the results obtained in the case of Pr3+:ZBLAN, a laser fluoride glass with much higher 4f5d absorption levels. Our investigation also gives the opportunity, in the case of Pr3+:YAG, to provide more complete and more reliable absorption and emission data than reported in the past literature and to exploit these data to better address the question of laser operation at various emission wavelengths. It is thus demonstrated that laser operation should be possible with improved laser performance at 488 nm, 616 nm and 744 nm, as it was already achieved in the past, but also at 566 nm and 931 nm by using appropriate laser cavities and laser mirrors.
summary: This paper addresses the inaccuracies of the standard Judd-Ofelt theory in predicting the spectroscopic properties of Pr3+:YAG. By implementing an extended theory that accounts for the 4f5d electronic configuration, the authors achieve much better agreement with experimental absorption and branching ratio data. The study provides more reliable spectroscopic data for this important laser crystal and identifies new potential wavelengths (566 nm and 931 nm) for laser operation.
main_problem: The standard Judd-Ofelt theory fails to accurately describe the luminescence properties of Pr3+ ions in YAG due to the influence of the nearby 4f5d excited electronic configuration and strict selection rules.
main_result: An extended Judd-Ofelt approach (specifically the EXT 1 model) provides a much better agreement with experimental absorption intensities and branching ratios, and predicts new potential laser operation wavelengths at 566 nm and 931 nm.
method: The authors combine experimental time-resolved spectroscopy and absorption measurements with an extended theoretical framework that relaxes standard selection rules and accounts for 4f5d configuration effects using Cowan's code.
model_or_system: The primary system is Pr3+ ions doped in YAG (Yttrium Aluminum Garnet) crystals, with comparisons made to Pr3+ in ZBLAN fluoride glass. The theoretical model uses an extended Judd-Ofelt theory involving three odd-rank parameters (X1, X3, X5).
key_observables: Absorption and emission intensities, fluorescence lifetimes, branching ratios, emission/gain cross-sections, and quantum emission efficiencies.
important_parameters: Judd-Ofelt parameters (Omega2, Omega4, Omega6), extended parameters (X1, X0, X5), 4f5d configuration energy (~34,000 cm-1), and temperature (77 K to 300 K).
main_assumptions: The EXT 1 model assumes a 'flat' or degenerate 4f5d excited configuration at a single energy level, while the EXT 2 model assumes a shift of free-ion energy levels.
figures_summary: The notes mention a table of absorption transition strengths using the EXT 2 approach and comparisons of gain cross-sections versus excited-state absorption.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the failure of standard Judd-Ofelt theory, presents an extended theoretical framework (KM, EXT 1, and EXT 2), validates these models against experimental absorption and emission data for Pr3+:YAG and Pr3+:ZBLAN, and concludes with predictions for new laser operating wavelengths.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 : Room temperature absorption (optical density) spectra of a 5.52 mm thick sample of 0.24%Pr :YAG crystal registered (a) in the visible and (b) the near- and mid-infrared spectral domains
Figure 2 : Time-resolved room temperature emission spectra of Pr3+ :YAG from the 3P0, 3P1 and 1I6 thermalized levels in the visible and near-infrared spectral range
Figure 3 : Time-resolved room temperature emission spectra of Pr3+ :YAG from the 1D2 emitting level in the visible and near-infrared spectral range
Figure 4 : Room (300 K) and low (77 K) temperature fluorescence decays (semi-log scale) of the 3P0 and 1D2 emitting levels of a 0.08at%Pr3+ :YAG crystal

Figure 5 : (a) 3H4  3P0 absorption and 3P0  3H4 emission cross section spectra derived using Reciprocity (RP) and Fuchtbauer-Ladenburg (FL) methods ; (b) Resulting gain cross section spectra for various excitation ratios 𝛽𝑒𝑥

Figure 6 : Relative standard deviations obtained between experimental and calculated transition (or oscillator) strengths in the case of Pr3+:YAG, by using the two « extended » EXT. 1 and EXT. 2 J.O. approaches, versus the 4f5d energy (lowest energy of the 4f5d excited configuration in the case of EXT. 2)

Figure 7 : Ratio between calculated and experimentally measured oscillator (or transition) strengths according to the different approaches (« modified » KM and « extended » EXT. 1 and EXT. 2 J.O. approaches) The results are about the same for the absorption transitions to levels 3H6, 3F2, 3F3,4 and 1D2 with an excellent agreement between calculated and measured values for the transitions to 3F2 and 3F3,4. The results are more satisfactory with the « extended » approaches than with the « modified » one in the case of the absorption transitions to the important levels 3P2, 3P0 and 3P1+1I6.

Figure 8 : Comparison of calculated and experimental branching ratios of the 3P0,1+1I6  A and 1D2  B emission transitions with A = 3H4, 3H5, 3H6, 3F2, 3F3,4, 1G4, 1D2 and B = 3H4, 3H5, 3H6, 3F2, 3F3,4, 1G4

Figure 9. Ratio between calculated and experimentally measured oscillator (or transition) strengths according to the different approaches (« Standard » and « Extended » EXT. 0, EXT. 1 and EXT. 2 J.O. approaches)
Figure 10 : Emission cross section spectra associated with the main emission transitions originating from the thermalized 3P0,1 and 1I6 energy levels.
